title: Intel Breaks Ground on Santa Clara Facility to Boost EUV Photomask Capacity for Future Nodes
gist: Intel broke ground on a Santa Clara EUV photomask facility for its 18A-P and 14A nodes, likely timed to capture a 35% Investment Tax Credit expiring end-2026, as its Arizona Fab 52 (18A) ramps to potentially 40,000 wafers/month while Ohio lags to 2030-plus.

themes.theme: Santa Clara photomask expansion and tax incentive

insights.claim: Intel broke ground on a 107,000-square-foot Bowers Campus expansion in Santa Clara comprising two three-story buildings to produce EUV photomasks (reticles) for its upcoming 18A-P and 14A process nodes.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: In-house EUV mask capacity supports Intel's leading-edge roadmap and reduces reliance on external mask supply for 14A/18A-P.
insights.quote: the facility will be used to produce EUV photomasks, or reticles, which are essential components for Intel's upcoming process nodes, including 18A-P and 14A
insights.locator: Details of the Santa Clara Expansion

insights.claim: Semiconductor projects must break ground before December 31, 2026 to qualify for a 35% Investment Tax Credit that disappears for projects launched in 2027 or later.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: The expiring 35% ITC is a hard deadline pulling forward fab groundbreakings across the industry in 2026; watch for a Q4 2026 rush.
insights.quote: semiconductor and advanced manufacturing projects must break ground before December 31, 2026, in order to qualify for a 35% Investment Tax Credit

insights.claim: Intel's Fab 52 at Ocotillo, Chandler, Arizona entered high-volume manufacturing in October 2025 as Intel's first 18A facility, starting with Panther Lake compute tiles and adding Clearwater Forest production later this year.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: 18A reaching HVM validates Intel's process roadmap execution, a key input to its foundry and product turnaround thesis.
insights.quote: The fab entered high-volume manufacturing in October 2025 — Intel's first facility to do so on the 18A node
insights.locator: Fab 52 and Intel's Broader Arizona Strategy

insights.claim: Fab 52 is built to handle more than 10,000 18A wafer starts per week, scaling to roughly 40,000 per month at full capacity, which would surpass the combined output of TSMC's Fab 21 Phase 1 and Phase 2 in Arizona.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Surpassing TSMC's Arizona output would materially shift domestic leading-edge capacity share toward Intel if realized.
insights.quote: which could scale to approximately 40,000 wafer starts per month at full capacity. That figure would surpass the combined output of TSMC's Fab 21 Phase 1 and Phase 2 facilities in Arizona

insights.claim: Intel's next Arizona buildout Fab 62 is expected to begin production around 2028 and could flexibly produce 14A if the Ohio site is further delayed or add 18A capacity if demand exceeds projections.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Fab 62's flexible node option is a hedge against Ohio delays; its 2028 timing is a milestone for Intel capacity planning.

insights.claim: Demand for Intel's foundry services is growing among major technology companies including Apple and NVIDIA.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Marquee foundry customers like Apple and NVIDIA would be a major validation of Intel's foundry turnaround if confirmed.
insights.quote: demand for its foundry services grows among major technology companies, including Apple and NVIDIA

insights.claim: Intel's Ohio facility continues to lag, with Phase 1 now expected complete by 2030 and operations starting 2030-2031, followed by Phase 2 production in 2032, versus a 2022 construction start on a $28 billion investment.
insights.speaker: SemiWiki
insights.so_what: Repeated Ohio slippage to 2030+ signals Intel's capacity growth is Arizona-centric near-term; discount Ohio in models.
insights.quote: Phase 1 is now expected to be completed by 2030, with operations starting between 2030 and 2031, followed by Phase 2 production in 2032
